title: Flow & StateFlow
description: Cold flows, hot flows, StateFlow, SharedFlow, operators, and testing
lesson:
Kotlin Flow: Reactive Streams

Flow is Kotlin's answer to reactive streams (like RxJava). It produces values asynchronously over time.

Cold vs Hot Flows

  • Cold flow (flow { }) — runs the producer code each time it's collected. Each collector gets its own emission. Like a vending machine: nothing happens until you press the button
  • Hot flow (StateFlow, SharedFlow) — emits values regardless of collectors. Like a radio: it broadcasts whether anyone is listening or not

Flow Builders

There are several ways to create a Flow:

// 1. flow { } — the primary builder. Emit values over time. fun fetchUsers(): Flow<User> = flow { val users = api.getUsers() users.forEach { emit(it) } // emit one at a time } // 2. flowOf() — create a flow from fixed values val colors = flowOf("red", "green", "blue") // 3. .asFlow() — convert collections/sequences to Flow val numbers = (1..10).asFlow() val listFlow = listOf("a", "b", "c").asFlow() // 4. callbackFlow { } — bridge callback APIs to Flow fun observeLocation(): Flow<Location> = callbackFlow { val callback = object : LocationCallback() { override fun onLocationResult(result: LocationResult) { trySend(result.lastLocation) // callback → Flow } } fusedClient.requestLocationUpdates(request, callback, Looper.getMainLooper()) awaitClose { // REQUIRED — cleanup when collector cancels fusedClient.removeLocationUpdates(callback) } }
callbackFlow is essential for bridging Android's callback-based APIs (LocationManager, SensorManager, ConnectivityManager, BroadcastReceiver) into reactive Flows. Always provide awaitClose { } to prevent resource leaks.

Key Operators — Transform & Filter

Flow operators are like assembly line stations — each transforms the stream:

// map — transform each emission val names: Flow<String> = userFlow.map { it.name } // filter — keep only matching emissions val adults: Flow<User> = userFlow.filter { it.age >= 18 } // onEach — side effect without transforming (great for logging) val logged = userFlow.onEach { Log.d("TAG", "User: $it") } // distinctUntilChanged — skip consecutive duplicates val unique = sensorFlow.distinctUntilChanged() // Emissions: 1, 1, 2, 2, 1 → 1, 2, 1 // take — collect only the first N emissions, then cancel val firstThree: Flow<User> = userFlow.take(3) // Collects exactly 3 items, then the flow completes // Useful for "give me the first N results" or pagination previews

Key Operators — Combine & Flatten

// combine — merge latest values from multiple flows // Emits whenever EITHER source emits val uiState = combine(queryFlow, filterFlow) { query, filter -> repository.search(query, filter) } // flatMapLatest — on new emission, cancel previous inner flow // Perfect for search: new keystroke cancels previous search val results = queryFlow.flatMapLatest { query -> repository.search(query) // returns Flow<List<Result>> }

Key Operators — Timing

// debounce — wait for pause in emissions // Great for search-as-you-type val debouncedQuery = queryFlow.debounce(300) // 300ms // User types: K(0ms) o(50ms) t(100ms) [pause 300ms] → emits "Kot"
Operator Cheat Sheet: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Transform: map, mapNotNull, transform Filter: filter, filterNotNull, distinctUntilChanged Combine: combine, zip, merge Flatten: flatMapLatest, flatMapConcat, flatMapMerge Timing: debounce, sample, timeout Error: catch, retry, retryWhen Side effect: onEach, onStart, onCompletion Convert: stateIn, shareIn, toList, first, single

Error Handling — catch & retry

// catch — handle upstream errors, emit fallback val users = repository.getUsersFlow() .catch { e -> Log.e("TAG", "Error: ${e.message}") emit(emptyList()) // emit fallback value } // retry — retry upstream on failure val data = api.streamData() .retry(retries = 3) { cause -> cause is IOException // only retry network errors } .catch { emit(CachedData) } // final fallback after retries exhausted
catch only catches upstream errors — exceptions thrown in collect { } are NOT caught by .catch { }. Place catch before collect, or use try/catch around the collect call.

StateFlow Deep Dive

StateFlow is a hot flow that always holds a current value. Think of it as reactive state storage.

// Mutable for internal writes, immutable for public reads private val _count = MutableStateFlow(0) val count: StateFlow<Int> = _count.asStateFlow() // Update atomically with .update { } fun increment() { _count.update { current -> current + 1 } // thread-safe } // ⚠️ .value = is NOT atomic for read-modify-write fun incrementUnsafe() { _count.value = _count.value + 1 // RACE CONDITION possible }
Key StateFlow behaviors:
1. Always has a value — initial value required at creation
2. Conflation — if value changes faster than collector can process, intermediate values are dropped (only latest is received)
3. Structural equality — setting the same value again is a no-op (uses equals())
4. Replay of 1 — new collectors immediately receive the current value

Converting Cold Flow to StateFlow with stateIn

// Convert a cold flow to a hot StateFlow val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = repository.observeData() .map { data -> UiState.Success(data) } .stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), // ★ initialValue = UiState.Loading )
SharingStarted Strategies: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Eagerly Start immediately, never stop → Background work continues forever → Use for: app-wide state that must always be fresh Lazily Start on first subscriber, never stop → Saves resources until needed, then runs forever → Use for: data that's expensive to start but cheap to keep WhileSubscribed Start on first sub, stop N ms after last unsubs (stopTimeoutMs) → WhileSubscribed(5000) = keeps alive 5s after last collector → Survives rotation (~1s) but stops when truly unused → ★ RECOMMENDED for most ViewModel StateFlows

SharedFlow — Events & Configurable Replay

SharedFlow is more flexible than StateFlow — no initial value required, configurable replay, no equality check:

// SharedFlow for one-time events (replay=0 → no replay on rotation) private val _events = MutableSharedFlow<UiEvent>() // default replay=0 val events: SharedFlow<UiEvent> = _events.asSharedFlow() fun showError(message: String) { viewModelScope.launch { _events.emit(UiEvent.ShowSnackbar(message)) } } // SharedFlow with buffer for high-throughput scenarios private val _analytics = MutableSharedFlow<AnalyticsEvent>( replay = 0, // don't replay past events extraBufferCapacity = 64, // buffer when collector is slow onBufferOverflow = BufferOverflow.DROP_OLDEST // drop old if full )
StateFlow vs SharedFlow: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ StateFlow SharedFlow ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Initial value Required Not required Replay Always 1 Configurable (0..N) Equality check Yes (skips dupes) No Best for UI STATE ONE-TIME EVENTS (loading, data, (navigation, snackbar, error screens) toast, dialog) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Rule: If the UI needs to "know the current state" → StateFlow If the UI needs to "react once and forget" → SharedFlow(replay=0)

Safe Collection — Lifecycle Awareness

Collecting flows in Android requires lifecycle awareness to avoid wasting resources when the app is backgrounded:

// ✅ BEST: In Compose — collectAsStateWithLifecycle @Composable fun UserScreen(viewModel: UserViewModel) { val state by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() // Stops collecting when lifecycle drops below STARTED } // ✅ In Activity/Fragment — repeatOnLifecycle lifecycleScope.launch { repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { viewModel.uiState.collect { state -> updateUI(state) } } // Collection stops in onStop, restarts in onStart } // ✅ Alternative: flowWithLifecycle (single flow) lifecycleScope.launch { viewModel.uiState .flowWithLifecycle(lifecycle, Lifecycle.State.STARTED) .collect { state -> updateUI(state) } }
Never use lifecycleScope.launch { flow.collect { } } without repeatOnLifecycle — the collection continues when the app is backgrounded, wasting battery on location updates, sensor data, or network polling. Always use lifecycle-aware collection.

Testing Flows with Turbine

Turbine (by Cash App) is the standard library for testing Kotlin Flows. It provides a .test { } extension that collects emissions and lets you assert them sequentially:

@Test fun searchViewModel_emitsLoadingThenResults() = runTest { val viewModel = SearchViewModel(FakeRepository()) viewModel.uiState.test { // Assert initial state assertEquals(UiState.Loading, awaitItem()) // Trigger action viewModel.search("kotlin") // Assert next emission val result = awaitItem() assertTrue(result is UiState.Success) // Cancel and ignore remaining events cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } @Test fun flow_emitsError() = runTest { val errorFlow = flow<String> { throw IOException("Network error") } errorFlow.test { val error = awaitError() // assert the flow throws assertTrue(error is IOException) } }
Turbine API summary:
awaitItem() — suspends until next emission, returns it
awaitError() — suspends until flow throws, returns the exception
awaitComplete() — suspends until flow completes
expectNoEvents() — asserts nothing was emitted
cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() — cleanup

Channel vs SharedFlow — When to Use Which

Both Channel and SharedFlow are hot, but they serve fundamentally different purposes. The key distinction: Channel is point-to-point, SharedFlow is broadcast.

Channel (point-to-point): SharedFlow (broadcast): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Producer → [Buffer] → Consumer A Producer → [Buffer] → Consumer A → Consumer B Each item delivered to ONE consumer → Consumer C Like a queue / work distribution Each item goes to ALL collectors Like a radio broadcast
// Channel — each item consumed by exactly ONE receiver // Perfect for work queues where you don't want double-processing val workChannel = Channel<DownloadTask>(capacity = Channel.BUFFERED) // Producer scope.launch { tasks.forEach { workChannel.send(it) } workChannel.close() // signal no more items } // Multiple workers — each task processed by ONE worker repeat(4) { workerId -> scope.launch { for (task in workChannel) { // iterates until closed processDownload(workerId, task) } } } // SharedFlow — each item received by ALL collectors // Perfect for events that multiple observers care about val eventBus = MutableSharedFlow<AppEvent>() // Both collectors receive EVERY event scope.launch { eventBus.collect { updateUI(it) } } scope.launch { eventBus.collect { logAnalytics(it) } }
Channel vs SharedFlow decision guide:
Use Channel when: each item should be processed by exactly one consumer (work queue, task distribution, actor pattern)
Use SharedFlow when: each item should be received by all observers (events, notifications, state broadcasts)
Use StateFlow when: you only care about the latest value (UI state)

Channel types: Channel.RENDEZVOUS (0 buffer, suspends until received), Channel.BUFFERED (default 64), Channel.UNLIMITED (no backpressure), Channel.CONFLATED (keeps only latest, like StateFlow)
Channels can leak if not closed. Always use produce { } (auto-closes on completion) or call channel.close() when done. Unlike SharedFlow, a Channel holds items in its buffer until consumed — if no one reads them, they sit in memory.

Channel for-loop — Go-style Iteration

Channels support a Go-like for loop that suspends until the next value arrives and exits when the channel is closed or the coroutine is cancelled:

// Go-style iteration over a channel val channel = Channel<String>() // Producer scope.launch { channel.send("A") channel.send("B") channel.send("C") channel.close() // signals no more items → for loop exits } // Consumer — suspends on each iteration until next value scope.launch { for (msg in channel) { process(msg) // A, B, C — then loop exits when channel closed } println("Done!") // reached after channel.close() } // ⚠️ If you NEVER close the channel: scope.launch { for (msg in channel) { // suspends forever after last item process(msg) // won't exit, won't spin, won't crash } // this line NEVER reached // The for loop just suspends waiting for the next send() // It only exits when: (1) channel.close() or (2) coroutine cancelled }
for (msg in channel) is syntactic sugar for while (!channel.isClosedForReceive) { val msg = channel.receive(); ... }. The loop body runs once per item, suspending between items. If the channel is never closed and the coroutine is never cancelled, the loop suspends indefinitely — it does NOT busy-wait or throw an exception.

Channel Buffer Types

Channel Capacity Constants: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ RENDEZVOUS (0) No buffer. send() suspends until a receiver calls receive(). Hand-to-hand delivery. → Use when: tight producer-consumer sync needed BUFFERED (64) Default buffer of 64 elements. send() only suspends when buffer is full. → Use when: general-purpose work queue UNLIMITED Infinite buffer (backed by LinkedList). send() never suspends. ⚠️ Can cause OOM. → Use when: producer must never block (fire-and-forget) CONFLATED Buffer of 1, newest value REPLACES old. send() never suspends. Slow receiver skips values. → Use when: only latest value matters (like StateFlow) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
// Creating channels with different buffer types val rendezvous = Channel<Int>() // RENDEZVOUS (default) val buffered = Channel<Int>(Channel.BUFFERED) // 64-element buffer val unlimited = Channel<Int>(Channel.UNLIMITED) // unbounded (OOM risk) val conflated = Channel<Int>(Channel.CONFLATED) // keeps only latest val custom = Channel<Int>(capacity = 10) // custom buffer size

send() vs trySend()

// send() — SUSPENDING. Waits if buffer is full. scope.launch { channel.send(value) // suspends until buffer has space } // trySend() — NON-SUSPENDING. Returns ChannelResult immediately. // Use in callbacks, onClick handlers, or anywhere you can't suspend. val result = channel.trySend(value) if (result.isSuccess) { // value was added to the buffer } else if (result.isFailure) { // buffer full — value was NOT sent Log.w("TAG", "Channel buffer full, dropping event") } else if (result.isClosed) { // channel was closed } // Real-world example: BroadcastReceiver → Channel val batteryChannel = Channel<Int>(Channel.CONFLATED) val receiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(context: Context, intent: Intent) { val level = intent.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_LEVEL, -1) batteryChannel.trySend(level) // can't suspend in onReceive! } }

produce { } Builder — Auto-Closing Channel

// produce { } creates a ReceiveChannel that auto-closes // when the producer coroutine completes or is cancelled. // Eliminates the "forgot to close" leak. fun CoroutineScope.produceNumbers(): ReceiveChannel<Int> = produce { var x = 1 while (true) { send(x++) // send to channel delay(100) // produce one every 100ms } // channel auto-closes when this block completes or scope cancels } // Usage val numbers = produceNumbers() repeat(5) { println(numbers.receive()) // 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 } numbers.cancel() // cancel the producer → channel closes // Pipeline pattern with produce: fun CoroutineScope.square(numbers: ReceiveChannel<Int>): ReceiveChannel<Int> = produce { for (x in numbers) { send(x * x) } } // Pipeline: numbers → square → consumer val nums = produceNumbers() val squares = square(nums) for (s in squares) { println(s) } // 1, 4, 9, 16, 25...
produce { } vs Channel(): Prefer produce { } whenever the producer is a single coroutine. It ties the channel lifecycle to the coroutine — no manual close() needed. Use Channel() directly when multiple producers send to the same channel, since produce only supports a single producer block.

Advanced Flow Patterns

Combining Multiple Flows for Complex UI State

// Real-world pattern: combine multiple data sources into one UI state class DashboardViewModel( private val userRepo: UserRepository, private val notifRepo: NotificationRepository, private val settingsRepo: SettingsRepository ) : ViewModel() { val uiState: StateFlow<DashboardState> = combine( userRepo.observeUser(), notifRepo.observeUnreadCount(), settingsRepo.observeTheme() ) { user, unreadCount, theme -> DashboardState( userName = user.name, unreadBadge = unreadCount, isDarkMode = theme == Theme.DARK ) }.stateIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), initialValue = DashboardState.Loading ) }

The shareIn Operator — Cold to Hot SharedFlow

// shareIn converts cold flow to SharedFlow (like stateIn for StateFlow) // Use when multiple collectors need the same upstream data val sharedLocationFlow: SharedFlow<Location> = locationProvider .observeLocation() .shareIn( scope = viewModelScope, started = SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(), replay = 1 // new collectors get the last known location ) // Without shareIn, each collector would start its own GPS listener!
Flow Conversion Summary: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Cold Flow ──stateIn()──→ StateFlow (always has .value) Cold Flow ──shareIn()──→ SharedFlow (configurable replay) MutableStateFlow ──.asStateFlow()──→ read-only StateFlow MutableSharedFlow──.asSharedFlow()──→ read-only SharedFlow Channel ──.receiveAsFlow()──→ cold Flow (point-to-point) Channel ──.consumeAsFlow()──→ cold Flow (consumes channel) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Collecting in Compose

// In a @Composable: val uiState by viewModel.uiState.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() // Lifecycle-aware: stops collecting when app is backgrounded

StateFlow vs SharedFlow vs LiveData

FeatureStateFlowSharedFlowLiveData
Initial valueRequiredNoNo
ReplaysLatest (1)ConfigurableLatest (1)
Equality checkYes (skips dupes)NoNo
Lifecycle awareNo (use collectAsStateWithLifecycle)NoYes
Android dependencyKotlin-onlyKotlin-onlyAndroid-only

questions.title: Cold vs Hot Flow
questions.description: What is the key difference between a cold Flow and a StateFlow?
options: Cold flows are faster than hot flows
A cold flow runs its producer code on each new collector. StateFlow is hot — it holds a current value and emits to all collectors regardless of when they start
Cold flows can only emit once, StateFlow emits multiple times
StateFlow is a Compose-only API
questions.explanation: A cold flow is lazy: the flow { } block only executes when collected, and each collector triggers a fresh execution. A hot StateFlow holds a current value (like LiveData), emits to all collectors, and skips duplicate values. Use cold flows for one-shot or per-request data; use StateFlow for UI state that multiple composables observe.
hints: Think: does the producer run without any collectors?
Cold = lazy/on-demand, Hot = always active

questions.title: Creating a StateFlow
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to expose a read-only StateFlow from a ViewModel.

blanks.hint: Property to update the current value

questions.explanation: The pattern: private MutableStateFlow for internal writes, public StateFlow (via .asStateFlow()) for external reads. This encapsulates mutations inside the ViewModel. Update via .value = newValue or .update { } for atomic read-modify-write.
hints: Same pattern as MutableLiveData/LiveData but with StateFlow
asStateFlow() prevents external code from modifying the state

questions.title: Search with debounce + flatMapLatest
questions.description: Trace through what happens when the user types "K", "Ko", "Kot" quickly, then pauses.
options: All three searches run: "K", "Ko", "Kot"
Only "Kot" triggers a search — debounce waits 300ms, "K" is filtered out (<2 chars), "Ko" is replaced by "Kot" via flatMapLatest
Only "K" runs because it was first
All are filtered out because they are too short
questions.explanation: Step by step: "K" and "Ko" and "Kot" arrive rapidly. debounce(300) waits for 300ms of silence — only "Kot" (the last value after the pause) passes through. filter { it.length >= 2 } passes "Kot" (length 3). flatMapLatest starts searching for "Kot" — if a new query arrived, it would cancel this search and start a new one. This is the canonical search-as-you-type pattern.
hints: debounce waits for a pause in emissions — rapid typing means only the last value survives
flatMapLatest cancels the previous search when a new query arrives

questions.title: Flow Not Lifecycle-Aware
questions.description: This flow collection continues even when the app is in the background, wasting battery on location updates. Fix it.

checks.message: Use collectAsStateWithLifecycle() which stops collecting when the app is not visible

questions.explanation: collectAsState() collects forever — even when the app is in the background. collectAsStateWithLifecycle() (from lifecycle-runtime-compose) stops collection when the lifecycle drops below STARTED (app backgrounded). This is critical for location, sensor data, or any continuous flow. Google recommends always using collectAsStateWithLifecycle in Compose.
hints: What happens to battery if you track GPS while the app is minimized?
There is a lifecycle-aware version of collectAsState

questions.title: StateFlow vs SharedFlow
questions.description: When should you use SharedFlow instead of StateFlow?
options: SharedFlow is always better — it replaces StateFlow
Use SharedFlow for one-time events (navigation, snackbars, toasts) that should not be replayed on configuration change
SharedFlow is for UI state, StateFlow is for events
Use SharedFlow only for testing
questions.explanation: StateFlow holds a current value and replays it to new collectors — perfect for UI state. SharedFlow (with replay=0) does NOT replay — perfect for one-time events like "show snackbar" or "navigate to screen". If you used StateFlow for events, rotating the phone would re-trigger the event. SharedFlow also doesn't skip duplicates and doesn't require an initial value.
hints: What happens if a "show error toast" StateFlow value is replayed after rotation?
One-time events vs ongoing state — different tools

questions.title: Combine Two Flows
questions.description: Write a ViewModel that combines a search query flow and a filter selection flow into a single UI state flow.

checks.message: Use combine() to merge multiple flows

questions.explanation: combine() merges the latest values from multiple flows — whenever EITHER changes, the transform runs with both latest values. stateIn() converts the cold combined flow into a hot StateFlow. WhileSubscribed(5000) keeps it active for 5 seconds after the last collector disappears (survives rotation).
hints: combine emits when ANY of its source flows emit
stateIn converts a cold flow to hot StateFlow with caching

questions.title: SharingStarted Strategies
questions.description: What does SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000) do when used with stateIn()?
options: Shares the flow for exactly 5 seconds
Starts the upstream flow when the first subscriber appears, stops it 5 seconds after the last subscriber disappears — surviving rotation but stopping when truly unused
Delays the first emission by 5 seconds
Replays the last 5 seconds of emissions
questions.explanation: WhileSubscribed(5000) means: start the upstream flow when there's at least one subscriber, and keep it running for 5000ms after the last subscriber disappears. The 5-second window lets it survive configuration changes (rotation takes ~1 second) without restarting the upstream. Compare: Eagerly (never stops), Lazily (starts on first sub, never stops), WhileSubscribed(0) (stops immediately).
hints: Think about what happens during screen rotation — the collector briefly disappears
5000ms is enough to survive a configuration change

questions.title: StateFlow Not Emitting Updates
questions.description: The UI never updates even though the ViewModel is updating the list. Find and fix the bug.

checks.message: Create a new list instance — StateFlow uses equality check and skips same reference

questions.explanation: StateFlow uses structural equality to skip duplicate emissions. Mutating the same list reference (.add(task)) does not change the reference — StateFlow sees the same object and skips the emission. You must create a new list instance. _tasks.update { it + task } creates a new list (the + operator returns a new list) and atomically updates the value. This is one of the most common StateFlow bugs.
hints: StateFlow checks if old value == new value. What happens when you mutate a list in place?
You need a new object reference, not a mutation of the existing one

questions.title: Channel for-loop Behavior
questions.description: What happens when you iterate over a Channel using for (msg in channel) and the channel is never closed?
options: The for loop throws a ClosedReceiveChannelException
The for loop exits immediately with an empty iteration
The for loop suspends indefinitely after processing all sent items, waiting for the next send() — it won't exit, spin, or crash
The for loop busy-waits, consuming CPU while polling for new items
questions.explanation: A for (msg in channel) loop suspends on each iteration waiting for the next item. If no more items are sent and the channel is never closed, the loop simply suspends forever — it does not busy-wait, throw, or exit. The loop only terminates when: (1) channel.close() is called, or (2) the coroutine running the loop is cancelled. This is safe and efficient — the suspended coroutine consumes no CPU.
hints: Think about what 'suspend' means — the coroutine is parked, not spinning
How does the for loop know when to exit? What signals 'no more items'?

questions.title: Channel vs SharedFlow Fan-Out
questions.description: A Channel has one producer sending items A, B, C, D. Two coroutines both run for (msg in channel) { process(msg) }. What happens?
options: Both coroutines receive all four items: A, B, C, D each
Each item is delivered to exactly one coroutine — e.g., coroutine 1 gets A, C and coroutine 2 gets B, D (fan-out distribution)
Only the first coroutine receives items; the second gets nothing
A ConcurrentModificationException is thrown
questions.explanation: Channel is point-to-point: each item is delivered to exactly one receiver. With two consumers, items are distributed (fan-out) — each item goes to whichever consumer calls receive() first. This is the work-queue pattern. If you need all consumers to see all items, use SharedFlow (broadcast). This is the fundamental difference: Channel = pipe (one reader), SharedFlow = radio (all listeners).
hints: Channel is like a queue — once an item is taken, it's gone
Compare with SharedFlow where every collector gets every emission
